ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Milo Peng packaged RTD; closest defensible match is chocolate-flavoured milk-based beverage (FOODON_03301099). Milo Peng is a malted chocolate malt beverage with condensed milk, served iced.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP used as closest defensible match for a ready-to-drink malted chocolate milk beverage. No exact FoodEx2 entry for Milo Peng packaged.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for packaged Milo Peng beverage; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard packaged serving of Milo Peng is approximately 200 ml (200 g), equivalent to one ready-to-drink carton or packet as commonly sold in Singapore school canteens and convenience stores.

nutrient_source.title: Milo Peng (packaged) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Milo Peng (packaged), cr_id D04020024. The dietary_fibre_g value of 36.88 g/100 g is retained as reported by HPB but flagged as a probable data entry error for a liquid beverage; it is not clinically credible and should be verified against the product label. Sodium is reported as 0.0 mg/100 g by HPB; this is confirmed zero per source. Iron is reported as 0.0 mg/100 g by HPB; confirmed zero per source. Saturated fat is not separately reported by HPB for this entry; set to -1. Monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, and trans fat not reported; set to -1. Potassium, cholesterol, vitamins, and other micronutrients not reported by HPB for this entry; set to -1.

gi_source.title: Milo (ready-to-drink, chocolate malt beverage) — GI analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entry for chocolate-flavoured milk-based beverages and Milo powder
gi_source.source_match_notes: No exact GI entry exists for Milo Peng packaged RTD in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on published GI values for Milo powder-based beverages and chocolate-flavoured milk drinks in the University of Sydney GI Database (GI range 34–55 for Milo-type beverages). Per the standard GI classification scale (low: ≤55, medium: 56–69, high: ≥70), a GI of 55 falls in the low category. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 20.0) / 100 = 11.0, using 200 g serving size and 10.0 g/100 g carbohydrate. Confidence is moderate given analogous estimation basis.

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar content and sweetened beverage classification
hypertension.guidance: Milo Peng (packaged) contains added sugar (5.0 g/100 g) and is classified as a sweetened beverage; individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid regular consumption as sweetened beverages are associated with increased cardiovascular risk and weight gain, both of which worsen blood pressure control. Sodium is reported as 0 mg/100 g per HPB, which is favourable, but the added sugar content remains a concern. The dietary fibre value of 36.88 g/100 g is a probable data entry error for a liquid beverage and should not be used to offset the sugar-related concern.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with added sugar and low-to-medium glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: Milo Peng (packaged) is a sugar-sweetened packaged beverage with added sugar (5.0 g/100 g) and a GI of approximately 55 (low boundary); individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this beverage as regular consumption of sweetened drinks is strongly associated with impaired glycaemic control and increased insulin resistance regardless of GI classification. The anomalous dietary fibre value of 36.88 g/100 g is a likely data error and does not confer any glycaemic benefit.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Added sugar and ultra-processed sweetened beverage classification
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Milo Peng (packaged) is an ultra-processed sweetened beverage; regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is associated with increased risk of cardiovascular events, dyslipidaemia, and weight gain, and individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should limit intake and prefer unsweetened alternatives. Fat content is low (1.68 g/100 g) and sodium is negligible per HPB data, but the added sugar and ultra-processed nature of the product remain cardiovascular risk factors.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar and absence of credible anti-inflammatory nutrients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Milo Peng (packaged) contains added sugar and provides no credible anti-inflammatory nutritional benefit; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid sweetened beverages as dietary sugar may promote systemic inflammation. The dietary fibre value of 36.88 g/100 g is a probable data anomaly and does not represent a genuine whole-food fibre source with anti-inflammatory properties.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Milo Peng (packaged) is a ready-to-drink iced chocolate malt beverage sold in Singapore, made from Milo powder (a malted barley and cocoa-based drink mix manufactured by Nestlé) combined with condensed milk and water, served chilled. It is a staple of Singapore school canteens, hawker centres, and convenience stores, and is deeply embedded in local food culture as an affordable, familiar sweet drink enjoyed by children and adults alike. The beverage is sweet, mildly chocolatey, and creamy due to the condensed milk component. Per 100 g, it provides approximately 63 kcal, 1.91 g protein, 1.68 g fat, and 10.0 g carbohydrate with 5.02 g sugar. Calcium is notably present at 120 mg/100 g, reflecting the milk content. Sodium is negligible at 0 mg/100 g per HPB data. The dietary fibre value of 36.88 g/100 g reported in the HPB source is almost certainly a data entry error for a liquid beverage and is not clinically credible; it should not be interpreted as a high-fibre food. Clinically, Milo Peng (packaged) is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) and is not recommended for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease due to its added sugar content and ultra-processed formulation. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 55 (low boundary per standard GI scale: low ≤55) based on analogous Milo-type beverage data from the University of Sydney GI Database. It is halal-certified and vegetarian but not vegan due to dairy content.

Is packaged Milo Peng suitable for hypertension?
What is the sugar content of Milo Peng?
Is Milo Peng good for children?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D04020024, confidence 0.95 per source). The dietary fibre anomaly is flagged but does not affect confidence in the remaining nutrient values. GI is an analogous estimate (moderate confidence) from the University of Sydney GI Database basis; overall record confidence is high for macros and moderate for GI.
notes: DIETARY FIBRE ANOMALY: The dietary_fibre_g value of 36.88 g/100 g is retained as reported by HPB Singapore Foodo (cr_id D04020024) but is almost certainly a data entry error for a liquid ready-to-drink beverage. A fibre content of 36.88 g/100 g is not physiologically plausible for a packaged Milo beverage and should be verified against the product label before any clinical or dietary use. Do not classify this food as high-fibre based on this value. The dietary_tags 'high_fibre' and 'wholegrain' from the input have been removed as they are not supported by credible evidence for this product. ZERO-VALUE FIELDS: sodium_mg is 0.0 mg/100 g — confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo source for this entry. iron_mg is 0.0 mg/100 g — confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo source for this entry. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES (-1): saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are all set to -1 as these values are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this food. GI CATEGORY CORRECTION: GI of 55 is classified as 'low' per the standard GI classification scale (low: ≤55, medium: 56–69, high: ≥70). Previous iteration incorrectly assigned gi_category 'medium'; corrected to 'low' in this iteration. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for Milo-type chocolate malt beverages; no exact RTD Milo Peng packaged entry exists in the GI database. Glycemic load per serving = (55 × 20.0 g carbohydrate per 200 g serving) / 100 = 11.0.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Built full RESULT JSON from input. Identified issues: dietary_tags 'high_fibre' and 'wholegrain' removed as unsupported. Sodium confirmed zero with documentation. Iron confirmed zero with documentation. All -1 sentinel fields documented in notes. GI source changed from 'Embedded GI Database' (forbidden) to University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate. Multilingual names added for Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, Hindi. health_context expanded to include all three required conditions (Hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, Cardiovascular Disease) with correct human_condition_name for Type 2 Diabetes. aliases_by_language populated and cross-checked against flat aliases array.

Iteration 2: Addressed validator feedback. Corrected foodon_match_type and foodex2_match_type from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent' to align with allowed values. Confirmed gi_category 'medium' was applied — however validator flagged this as incorrect.

Iteration 3: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low'. Per the standard GI classification scale (low: ≤55, medium: 56–69, high: ≥70), a GI value of exactly 55 falls in the low category, not medium. Updated gi_source source_match_notes and notes field to document this correction. Updated health_context type2_diabetes key_factor to reflect 'low-to-medium glycemic index' for clinical accuracy. Updated llm_training natural_language_description to reflect the corrected GI category.
